text: We describe in this video the G-pouch microvascularization assessment using the knee infrared technology. We will perform three different assessments. The first one after we're building the pouch, the second after the elongation procedure if needed, and the final product at the end of the surgery after the G-pouch has been performed. We use a near-infrared scope, and then we will inject three milliliters of endocyanin green IV. The signal will appear on the screen in thirty seconds. As you can see on that picture, there is a tree square on the left. The first one is a normal view. The second one, black and white, is a near-infrared view. And the third one is an enhanced reality view. it is easily possible to change the large screen according to the different screen on the left.

The surgeon frees the small bowel mesentery to maximize its length, preparing for the confection of a G-pouch after coloprotectomy. The vascular supply of the pouch is clamped using a vascular clamp to assess microvascularization and ensure sufficient length for anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a near-infrared scope to assess the blood supply to the bottom end of the G-pouch. The screen displays three views: normal light, infrared light, and enhanced reality. The black and white infrared view confirms preserved vascular supply post-elongation procedure, allowing safe division of vessels and positioning of the pouch for anastomosis. After anastomosis, the intra-abdominal view shows the joint at the top end, and injected endocyanin highlights the microvessels up to the anastomosis level.

The surgeon performs an endoanal view of the G-pouch, inspecting the interior for signs of bleeding and assessing vascularization. The stable line is clearly visible, allowing for precise localization and stitching of any bleeding areas. If the pouch is well vascularized and no bleeding is observed, the surgeon proceeds to perform the ileostomy and safely concludes the operation.
In conclusion, the use of an ear infrared scope in GPOW surgery is highly feasible and adds safety about the patch blood supply at the time of surgery.
